title: AI Trust & Safety Digest
tagline: A curated current snapshot of AI trust, safety, and governance, including as many materially important fresh items as the day warrants.
lead: Today’s signal is about operationalizing AI oversight: one thread pushes toward more credible external evaluations, another formalizes frontier governance against emerging regulation, and a third expands public-sector measurement infrastructure. The mix suggests trust and safety work is moving from broad principles toward concrete evaluation methods, access controls, privacy-preserving deployment, and institutional coordination.

items.title: OpenAI outlines a shared playbook for trustworthy third-party evaluations
items.why_it_matters: It could standardize how external evaluators test frontier model capabilities and safeguards.

items.title: OpenAI publishes Frontier Governance Framework
items.why_it_matters: It clarifies how one frontier lab maps safety and security practices to emerging regulatory requirements.

items.title: NIST expands AI consortium scope and seeks new members
items.why_it_matters: Broader measurement and evaluation work at NIST can shape shared standards for AI assessment and governance.

items.title: Google details private analytics via zero-trust aggregation
items.why_it_matters: Privacy-preserving analytics techniques can reduce data exposure in AI and security-sensitive deployments.

items.title: OpenAI launches Rosalind Biodefense trusted-access program
items.why_it_matters: Vetted-access controls for high-stakes bio applications show how labs may gate sensitive model use.

note: Selected from recent trust-and-safety relevant posts and papers by labs, evaluators, standards bodies, policy organizations, and arXiv, then summarized with GPT-5.4. Includes as many materially important fresh items as the day warrants.
